mappings.rationaleAI: The evolutionary process of human divergence from ancestral primates (A), distinguishing characteristics of the Hominidae including bipedalism and encephalization (B.2), and the fossil record from Australopithecus through Homo sapiens (C).
Homo sapiens defined by bipedality, manual dexterity, and advanced cognition as distinguishing Hominidae characteristics (B.2), with evolutionary origins ~300,000 years ago in Africa (C.2.d).
Evolution as the overarching process of change in heritable characteristics across generations, providing the framework for understanding human evolutionary relationships (A).
Darwin's theory of natural selection and The Descent of Man (1871) directly addressing human evolution as part of the evolutionary process (A).

Primates as the order including prosimians and anthropoids (B.1.d), with distinguishing characteristics including enlarged brains and opposable thumbs (B.1.a) and their evolutionary paleontology (B.1.c).
Ecological relationships relevant to the natural history of primate life (B.1.b).
Anthropology including physical anthropology and archaeology relevant to the discovery and recognition of the hominid fossil record (C.1).
The origin of life as the deepest context for the evolutionary process from which human evolution eventually proceeded (A).
Paleontology providing the fossil evidence for primate evolution (B.1.c) and the hominid fossil record (C).
Africa as the cradle of Homo sapiens where anatomically modern humans emerged ~300,000 years ago (C.2.d).
The Stone Age encompassing hominid evolution and lithic tool use from Oldowan tools through the Neolithic, relevant to classification of the Hominidae (C.2).
The 3.7-billion-year history of life providing evolutionary context (A) and primate evolution within the broader mammalian radiation (B.1.c).
Biological taxonomy providing the classification framework for primates (B.1.d) and the classification of the Hominidae (C.2).
Rodents as the largest mammalian order providing comparative context for primate evolution and paleontology (B.1.c).
Dinosaurs' Cretaceous-Paleogene extinction enabling mammalian radiation, providing evolutionary context for the process leading to primate and human emergence (A).
Natural selection as the primary mechanism driving the evolutionary process (A).
Biology as the overarching science whose evolutionary framework underlies understanding of the evolutionary process (A).
Reproductive strategies as inferred behavioral characteristics of the Hominidae (B.2.b).
Animals as the kingdom within which primates are classified, relevant to primate characteristics and evolutionary context (B.1).
Species concepts relevant to the classification of Hominidae including Australopithecus and Homo species (C.2).
Extinction relevant to the disappearance of Neanderthals and other archaic Homo species (C.2.d.ii).
Inferred behavioral characteristics of the Hominidae including tool use and social organization (B.2.b).
